title: Federal Power Commission

infobox.value: (**FPC**) was an [independent commission](/wiki/Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_government "Independent agencies of the United States government") of the [United States government](/wiki/United_States_government "United States government"), originally organized on June 23, 1930, with five members nominated by the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and confirmed by the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). The FPC was originally created in 1920 by the [Federal Water Power Act](/wiki/Federal_Power_Act "Federal Power Act"), which provided for the licensing by the FPC of [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectric "Hydroelectric") projects on the land or [navigable](/wiki/Navigable "Navigable") water owned by the federal government. The FPC has since been replaced by the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

US government body (1930–1977)

Federal Power Commission

| Agency overview | |
| --- | --- |
| Formed | June 23, 1930 (1930-06-23) |
| Dissolved | October 1, 1977 |
| Superseding agency | * [FERC](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission") |
| Jurisdiction | [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") |
| Agency executive | * [Charles B. Curtis](/wiki/Charles_B._Curtis "Charles B. Curtis"), Chairman (*last*) |

The **Federal Power Commission** (**FPC**) was an [independent commission](/wiki/Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_government "Independent agencies of the United States government") of the [United States government](/wiki/United_States_government "United States government"), originally organized on June 23, 1930, with five members nominated by the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and confirmed by the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). The FPC was originally created in 1920 by the [Federal Water Power Act](/wiki/Federal_Power_Act "Federal Power Act"), which provided for the licensing by the FPC of [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectric "Hydroelectric") projects on the land or [navigable](/wiki/Navigable "Navigable") water owned by the federal government. The FPC has since been replaced by the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission").

The FPC also regulated interstate electric utilities and the [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") industry.

In June 1939, President Roosevelt appointed [Leland Olds](/wiki/Leland_Olds "Leland Olds") to the FPC, who served as chairman from January 1940 until 1949. Under Olds’ leadership, the FPC successfully pressured electric utilities to extend power into neglected rural areas and to lower electricity rates to increase use. However his reappointment in 1949 [failed in the Senate](/wiki/Leland_Olds_hearing "Leland Olds hearing") due to concerns about his suspected previous sympathy to [communism](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA").

[James G. Watt](/wiki/James_G._Watt "James G. Watt") was another prominent FPC commissioner, who conducted prayer meetings prior to the FPC sessions.

## Office of Chairman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Power_Commission&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Office of Chairman")]

From its founding in 1920 until its first reform in 1930, the FPC did not have its own commissioners; rather, it was chaired [ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio") by the [Secretaries of War](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_War "United States Secretary of War"), [Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior"), and [Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Agriculture "United States Secretary of Agriculture").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The first "Chairman" in that sense was Woodrow Wilson's Secretary of War, [Newton D. Baker](/wiki/Newton_D._Baker "Newton D. Baker"). Before 1930, five Secretaries of War, five Secretaries of the Interior, and five Secretaries of Agriculture held title at the FPC.

Following the 1930 reforms, FPC had its own commissioners, with the following commissioners holding the title of Chairman of the Federal
Power Commission:

Source:[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Relevant laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Power_Commission&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Relevant laws")]

* 46 Stat. 797
* 41 Stat. 1063
* 16 [United States Code](/wiki/United_States_Code "United States Code") 791-823, the Federal Water Power Act

## Dissolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Power_Commission&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Dissolution")]

Main article: [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission")

On October 1, 1977, the FPC was replaced by the [Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission").[[4]](#cite_note-4)
